title: Variational Quantum Models for Knowledge Graph Embeddings on NISQ Devices
authors: Guido Bellomo, Martín Santesteban, Patricio Bruno, Santiago Cifuentes, Gustavo Martín Bosyk
abstract: Variational Quantum Algorithms (VQAs) combine quantum circuits with classical optimization to tackle problems that may benefit from the capabilities of near-term quantum hardware. In knowledge graph embedding, recent proposals based on this approach follow a similar overall architecture but differ in the way they compute the score function and in the number of qubits they require. One design uses $n+1$ qubits and obtains the score through a switch test on an ancillary qubit, while another employs $2n+1$ qubits and applies a swap test between two registers. In both cases, entities and relations are represented in a Hilbert space of dimension $d = 2^n$, with comparable computational cost and the same mean squared error loss. This work introduces a unified framework that captures the two schemes and makes it possible to explore new variants. Within this setting, we propose an alternative that keeps the intuitive meaning of the score function while dispensing with ancillary qubits and entangled measurements. The result is a model better suited to current NISQ devices, reducing hardware demands without sacrificing interpretability.
summary: The paper proposes a new way to perform knowledge graph embeddings on near-term quantum computers. By using a 'compute-uncompute' method, the authors reduce the number of qubits and the complexity of measurements required. This makes the algorithm more compatible with current noisy quantum hardware.
main_problem: Reducing the hardware demands, such as qubit overhead and circuit depth, for Variational Quantum Algorithms (VQAs) used in Knowledge Graph Embedding on NISQ devices.
main_result: The introduction of the qCUERO framework and a new 'compute-uncompute' model that eliminates the need for ancillary qubits and entangled measurements while maintaining an interpretable score function.
method: A hybrid quantum-classical pipeline using a compute-uncompute strategy where the score is estimated from the probability of the all-zero outcome in a computational basis measurement.
model_or_system: A variational quantum model where entities are represented as quantum states in a Hilbert space of dimension 2^n and relations are represented as unitary operations.
key_observables: The score function (delta_hrt) representing the plausibility of a triple (head, relation, tail).
important_parameters: Trainable parameters for entities (theta_e) and relations (theta_r); Hilbert space dimension d = 2^n.
main_assumptions: The study is primarily theoretical; the impact of quantum noise and performance on real-world knowledge graphs are yet to be fully validated.
figures_summary: Fig 1 compares existing Switch and Swap test architectures; Fig 2 shows the general hybrid quantum-classical pipeline; Fig 3 illustrates the proposed compute-uncompute architecture.
paper_structure: The paper introduces a unified framework (qCUERO), analyzes existing architectures (Switch and Swap tests), proposes a new compute-uncompute model, and discusses trade-offs regarding circuit depth and error robustness.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: Variational quantum circuit architectures for knowledge graph embedding. (a) Circuit implementing the SWITCH test to estimate the score function. (b) Circuit implementing the standard SWAP test for score computation.

Fig. 2: General hybrid quantum–classical pipeline for VQA- based knowledge graph embedding. (i) Classical inputs are encoded into a parameterized quantum circuit that computes a score; (ii) a loss function is evaluated classically using the predicted scores and target values; (iii) a classical optimizer updates the parameters of the quantum ansatz. Dashed lines indicate classical computation steps.
Fig. 3: Quantum circuit architecture for the proposed VQA model, using a compute–uncompute scheme to evaluate the score without ancillary qubits.
